steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence question. The claim to support is that high-speed rail is more cost-effective than highway systems. The correct answer must provide direct evidence that rail delivers more value per dollar than highways do — not discuss rail's implementation challenges, funding needs, or travelers' preferences.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘In most locations in the US, increasing highway capacity is more expensive relative to the volume of traffic that can be accommodated than increasing high-speed rail capacity is.’: directly compares cost per unit of traffic volume, showing rail gets more capacity per dollar than highways do. This is a textbook cost-effectiveness comparison. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘In much of the US, the existing rail infrastructure is so old that it would have to be replaced entirely in order to accommodate high-speed trains.’: describes an implementation barrier, not a cost advantage. It actually suggests rail could be more expensive to build. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Building successful high-speed rail infrastructure would require that the US government establish a fund to maintain the rail lines long-term.’: addresses ongoing funding needs, not whether rail is more cost-effective than highways. Neutral at best, a liability at worst. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Unlike people in the United States, people in Turkey and Germany are less interested in traveling by highway than they are in traveling by high-speed rail.’: discusses travel preferences in other countries, not cost-effectiveness in the US. Irrelevant to the claim. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
